solutions.solution:
B. If a given statement fits as a punchline, what is it, idea or wavelength?

When you examine the two suggested statements and find that both of them fit as punchlines, you will have to further find whether both of them fit the same sense (same wavelength of approach) or contrasting ideas, i.e., trading wavelengths of approach. You will be able to answer this question only if you follow the idea or logic behind a punchline.

This is an easy job and can be accomplished quickly if you ask yourself one question: Whether makes it funny? This will automatically lead you to the logic of the punchline.

For example, consider Ex 12 to Ex 15 above. What makes reply I of Ex 11 funny? It is the indirect way in which the nature of employers has been criticised, which makes it funny. What about Ex 13? It is the juxtaposing of a serious thing (going abroad) while reading about P which makes it funny. What about reply I of Ex 14? It is funny because B takes a dig at A's stupidity and indirectly says that A's head is empty. Finally, we come to Ex 15. Here both the statements fit as punchlines. How? Reply I fits because it implies that the patient is still deaf and reply II fits because it implies that the patient is improving and worried about his bills than about his deafness.
